title: How to Use the IF Function in Excel (with Examples)
dek: The IF function returns one value when a condition is true and another when it is false. The syntax is =IF(logical_test, value_if_true, value_if_false). Here is how to use it, nest it, and avoid the usual mistakes.

intent.primaryKeyword: how to use if function in excel

seo.metaTitle: How to Use the IF Function in Excel (Examples)
seo.metaDescription: Learn the Excel IF function: syntax, real examples, nested IFs, and how to combine it with AND and OR — with common errors explained.

richText: The IF function is how you make Excel decide. It checks a condition and returns one answer if it is true and a different answer if it is false — the building block of nearly every smart spreadsheet.

body.caption: The three parts of IF
rows: The condition to check, e.g. B2>100
What to return when the condition is met
What to return when it is not

richText: To label sales in B2 as "Pass" or "Fail" based on a target of 100:

body.richText: Text values must go in double quotes. If you want a number instead, leave the quotes off, for example =IF(B2>=100, 1, 0).

body.text: Nesting IF and combining with AND / OR

richText: You can put an IF inside another IF for more than two outcomes, or use AND and OR to test several conditions at once, like =IF(AND(B2>=100, C2="VIP"), "Bonus", "Standard"). If you have Microsoft 365, the IFS function is cleaner than deeply nested IFs.

body.richText: A blank cell counts as 0 in a numeric test, which can produce a surprising "true". Wrap the test in another IF that checks for blanks if that matters for your data.

. And if an IF returns #VALUE!, see

items.question: Can the IF function check text?
items.answerRichText: Yes. Use quotes around the text, for example =IF(A2="Yes", 1, 0). Text comparisons in Excel are not case-sensitive, so "yes" and "YES" are treated the same.

items.question: How many IFs can you nest?
items.answerRichText: Excel allows up to 64 nested IFs, but anything beyond three or four becomes hard to read. Use IFS, VLOOKUP against a lookup table, or the SWITCH function instead for many outcomes.
